53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
157	
158	## Rules
159	
160	### What you CAN do
161	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
162	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
163	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
164	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
165	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
166	
167	### What you MUST do
168	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
169	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
170	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
171	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
172	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
173	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
174	
175	### What you MUST NOT do
176	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
177	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
178	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
179	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
180	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
181	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
182	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
183	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
184	
185	## Reading references
186	
187	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
188	
189	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
190	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
191	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
192	
193	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
194	
195	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
196	
197	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
198	
199	Conditions for dispatch:
200	
201	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
202	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
203	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
204	
205	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
206	
207	```
208	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
209	  --name reference-retriever \
210	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
211	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
212	  --write-domain 'references/**'
213	```
214	
215	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
216	
217	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
218	
219	## Workflow
220	
221	1. Read your directive completely.
222	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
223	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
224	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
225	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
226	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
227	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.

112	**Main theorem proof strategy**: Riemann–Roch for genus 0 is the case where Serre duality collapses (the canonical divisor $K_C$ has $\deg K_C = -2 < 0$ on $\mathbb P^1$, so $\ell(K_C - D) = 0$ for $\deg D \geq 0$). 32	\section{{\v C}ech cohomology of quasi-coherent sheaves}
33	\label{section-cech-quasi-coherent}
34	
35	\noindent
36	Let $X$ be a scheme.
37	Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
38	Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
39	of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
40	where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
41	the unit ideal, see
42	Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}.
43	
44	\begin{lemma}
45	\label{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
46	Let $X$ be a scheme.
47	Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
48	Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
49	open covering of an affine open of $X$.
50	Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
51	all $p > 0$.
52	\end{lemma}
53	
54	\begin{proof}
55	Write $U = \Spec(A)$ for some ring $A$.
56	In other words, $f_1, \ldots, f_n$ are elements of $A$
57	which generate the unit ideal of $A$.
58	Write $\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}$ for some $A$-module $M$.
59	Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
60	$\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$

1699

1706	\centerline{\large\bf Lemma on Generic Flatness}
1707	
1708	\medskip
1709	
1710	
1711	\begin{lemma} \label{algebraic lemma for generic flatness}
1712	Let $A$ be a noetherian domain, 
1713	and $B$ a finite type $A$ algebra. Let $M$ be a finite $B$-module. 
1714	Then there exists an $f\in A$, $f\ne 0$, such that 
1715	the localisation $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$.
1716	\end{lemma}
1717	
1718	
1719	\proof Over the quotient field $K$ of $A$, the $K$-algebra 
1720	$B_K = K\otimes _AB$ is of finite type, and $M_K = K\otimes_AM$ is a 
1721	finite module over $B_K$. Let $n$ be the dimension of the support
1722	of $M_K$ over $\spec B_K$. We argue by induction on $n$, starting
1723	with $n=-1$ which is the case when $M_K =0$. In this case, as
1724	$K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$, each $v\in M$
1725	is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
1726	generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating 
1727	elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$.
1728	Hence $M_f =0$, proving the lemma when $n=-1$.
1729	
1730	Now let $n\ge 0$, and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
1731	As $B$ is noetherian and $M$ is assumed to be a finite $B$-module,
1732	there exists a finite filtration
1733	$$0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M$$
1734	where each $M_i$ is a $B$-submodule of $M$ such that for each $i\ge 1$
1735	the quotient module $M_i/M_{i-1}$ is isomorphic to $B/\pp_i$ for 
1736	some prime ideal $\pp_i$ in $B$. 
1737	
1738	Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact
1739	sequence of $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of
1740	$A$ such that $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over
1741	respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$, then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$
1742	where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact repeatedly.
1743	Therefore it is enough to prove the result when 
1744	$M$ is of the form $B/\pp$ for a prime ideal $\pp$ in $B$. 
1745	This reduces us to the case where $B$ is a domain and $M=B$.
1746	
1747	As by assumption $K\otimes_AB$ has dimension $n\ge 0$ (that is,
1748	$K\otimes_AB$ is non-zero), the map $A\to B$ must be injective.
1749	By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements 
1750	$b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$, 
1751	such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra
1752	$K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and 
1753	the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent
1754	over $K$. (For simplicity of notation, we write $1\otimes b$ simply as $b$.)
1755	If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for  
1756	coefficients of equations of integral dependence 
1757	satisfied by a finite set of
1758	algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, 
1759	we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. 
1760	
1761	Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
1762	domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
1763	sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1764	$$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
1765	where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$.
1766	Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
1767	as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
1768	induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$), 
1769	there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$.
1770	Taking $f=gh$, the lemma follows from the 
1771	above short exact sequence.
1772	\hfill$\square$
1773	
1774	
1775	
1776	The above theorem has the following consequence, which follows
1777	by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of $S$.
1778	
1779	
1780	
1781	\begin{theorem}\label{generic flatness} 
1782	Let $S$ be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1783	Let $p: X\to S$ be a finite type morphism, and let $\F$ be a 
1784	coherent sheaf of ${\mathcal O}_X$-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1785	open subscheme $U\subset S$ such that the restriction of $\F$ 
1786	to $X_U = p^{-1}(U)$ is flat over ${\mathcal O}_U$.
1787	\end{theorem}
1788	
1789

1807	\centerline{\large\bf Existence of Flattening Stratification}
1808	
1809	\medskip
1810	
1811	\begin{theorem}\label{flattening stratification} 
1812	Let $S$ be a noetherian scheme, and let 
1813	$\F$ be a coherent sheaf on the projective space $\PP^n_S$
1814	over $S$. Then the set $I$ of Hilbert polynomials of
1815	restrictions of $\F$ to fibers of $\PP^n_S\to S$
1816	is a finite set. Moreover, for each $f\in I$ there exist 
1817	a locally closed subscheme $S_f$ of $S$,  
1818	such that the following conditions are satisfied.
1819	
1820	\textbf{(i) Point-set: } The underlying set $|S_f|$ of $S_f$ consists of 
1821	all points $s\in S$ where the Hilbert polynomial of the
1822	restriction of $\F$ to $\PP^n_s$ is $f$. In particular, 
1823	the subsets $|S_f|\subset |S|$ are disjoint, 
1824	and their set-theoretic union is $|S|$.
1825	
1826	\textbf{(ii) Universal property: } 
1827	Let $S'=\coprod S_f$ be the coproduct of the $S_f$, and let
1828	$i:S'\to S$ be the morphism induced by the inclusions
1829	$S_f\hra S$. Then the sheaf $i^*(\F)$ on $\PP^n_{S'}$ 
1830	is flat over $S'$. Moreover, $i:S'\to S$ has the universal property that 
1831	for any morphism $\varphi :T\to S$ the pullback $\varphi^*(\F)$ on
1832	$\PP^n_T$ is flat over $T$ if and only if $\varphi$ factors through
1833	$i:S'\to S$.  The subscheme $S_f$ is 
1834	uniquely determined by the polynomial $f$.
1835	
1836	\textbf{(iii) Closure of strata: } 
1837	Let the set $I$ of Hilbert polynomials be given a total 
1838	ordering, defined by putting $f<g$ whenever $f(n) < g(n)$ for all
1839	$n \gg  0$. Then the closure in $S$ of the subset $|S_f|$ 
1840	is contained in the union of all $|S_g|$ where $f\le g$. 
1841	\end{theorem}
1842	
1843	
1844	\proof It is enough to prove the theorem for open subschemes of
1845	$S$ which cover $S$, as
1846	the resulting strata will then glue together by their universal property. 
1847	
1848	
1849	\textbf{Special case: } Let $n=0$, so that $\PP^n_S=S$. 
1850	For any $s\in S$, the \textbf{fiber} $\F|_s$ of $\F$ over $s$
1851	will mean the pull-back of $\F$ to the subscheme
1852	$\spec \kappa(s)$, where $\kappa(s)$ is the residue field at $s$. 
1853	(This is obtained by tensoring the stalk of $\F$ at $s$ with the
1854	residue field at $s$, both regarded as ${\mathcal O}_{S,s}$-modules.)
1855	The Hilbert polynomial of the 
1856	restriction of $\F$ to the fiber over $s$ is the degree $0$ 
1857	polynomial $e \in \Q[\lambda]$, where $e = \dim_{\kappa(s)}\F|_s$. 
1858	
1859	By Nakayama lemma, any basis of $\F|_s$ prolongs to a neighbourhood $U$ of $s$ 
1860	to give a set of generators for $F|_U$. Repeating this argument,
1861	we see that there exists a smaller neighbourhood $V$ of $s$ in which
1862	there is a right-exact sequence
1863	$${\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus m} \stackrel{\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus e} 
1864	\stackrel{\phi}{\to} \F \to 0$$
1865	Let $I_e\subset {\mathcal O}_V$ be the ideal sheaf
1866	formed by the entries of the $e\times m$ matrix
1867	$(\psi_{i,j})$ of the homomorphism 
1868	${\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus m} \stackrel{\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus e}$. 
1869	Let $V_e$ be the closed subscheme of $V$ defined by $I_e$. 
1870	For any morphism of schemes
1871	$f: T\to V$, the pull-back sequence 
1872	$${\mathcal O}_T^{\oplus m} \stackrel{f^*\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_T^{\oplus e} 
1873	\stackrel{f^*\phi}{\to}f^* \F \to 0$$
1874	is exact, by right-exactness of tensor products. 
1875	Hence the pull-back $f^*\F$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_T$-module of
1876	rank $e$ if and only if $f^*\psi =0$, that is, 
1877	$f$ factors via the subscheme $V_e\hra V$ defined by the vanishing of all
1878	entries $\psi_{i,j}$.
1879	Thus we have proved assertions \textbf{(i)} and \textbf{(ii)} of the theorem.
1880	
1881	
1882	As the rank of the matrix $(\psi_{i,j})$ is lower semi-continuous,
1883	it follows that the function $e$ is upper semi-continuous, which
1884	proves the assertion \textbf{(iii)} of the theorem, completing its proof
1885	when $n=0$. 
1886	
1887	
1888	\textbf{General case: } We now allow the integer $n$ to be arbitrary.
1889	The idea of the proof is as follows:  
1890	We show the existence of a stratification
1891	of $S$ which is a `g.c.d.' of the flattening
1892	stratifications for direct images $\pi_*\F(i)$ for all $i \ge N$ for some
1893	integer $N$ (where the flattening
1894	stratifications for $\pi_*\F(i)$ exist by case $n=0$ which we have 
1895	treated above). This is the desired flattening stratification of $\F$
1896	over $S$, as follows from Lemma \ref{graded module is flat}.
1897	
1898	
1899	As $S$ is noetherian, it is a finite union of irreducible components,
1900	and these are closed in $S$.
1901	Let $Y$ be an irreducible component of $S$, and let $U$ be the
1902	non-empty open subset of $Y$ which consists of all points which do not 
1903	lie on any other irreducible component of $S$. 
1904	Let $U$ be given the reduced subscheme
1905	structure. Note that this makes $U$ an integral scheme, which is a
1906	locally closed subscheme of $S$. 
1907	By Theorem \ref{generic flatness} on generic flatness, 
1908	$U$ has a non-empty open subscheme $V$ such that
1909	the restriction of $\F$ to $\PP^n_V$ is flat over ${\mathcal O}_V$.
1910	Now repeating the argument with $S$ replaced by its reduced 
1911	closed subscheme $S-V$,
1912	it follows by noetherian induction on $S$ that there exist
1913	finitely many reduced, locally closed, mutually disjoint subschemes
1914	$V_i$ of $S$ such that set-theoretically 
1915	$|S|$ is the union of the $|V_i|$ and 
1916	the restriction of $\F$ to $\PP^n_{V_i}$ is flat over ${\mathcal O}_{V_i}$.
1917	As each $V_i$ is a noetherian scheme, and as the Hilbert polynomials
1918	are locally constant for a flat family of sheaves, it follows 
1919	that only finitely many polynomials occur in $V_i$
1920	in the family of Hilbert polynomials $P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))$ 
1921	as $s$ varies over points of $V_i$. This allows us to conclude 
1922	the following:
1923	
1924	\medskip
1925	
1926	%\statement\label{finitely many Hilbert polynomials} 
1927	\textbf{(A) } Only finitely many distinct Hilbert polynomials 
1928	$P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))$ occur, as $s$ 
1929	varies over all of $S$.
1930	
1931	\medskip
1932	
1933	By the semi-continuity theorem applied to the flat families 
1934	$\F_{V_i} = \F|_{\PP^n_{V_i}}$ para\-met\-rised by the 
1935	finitely many noetherian schemes 
1936	$V_i$, we get the following: 
1937	
1938	
1939	
1940	\medskip
1941	%\statement\label{uniform vanishing of higher cohomology} 
1942	\textbf{(B) } There exists an integer $N_1$ such that 
1943	$R^r\pi_*\F(m)=0$ for all $r\ge 1$ and $m\ge N_1$,
1944	and moreover $H^r(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m)) = 0$
1945	for all $s\in S$.
1946	
1947	
1948	\medskip
1949	
1950	
1951	
1952	For each $V_i$, by Lemma \ref{base change without flatness}
1953	there exists an
1954	integer $r_i \ge N_1$ with the property
1955	that for any $m\ge r_i$ the base change homomorphism
1956	$$(\pi_*\F(m))|_{V_i} \to {\pi_i}_*\F_{V_i}(m)$$ 
1957	is an isomorphism, where $\F_{V_i}$ denotes the restriction of
1958	$\F$ to $\PP^n_{V_i}$, and $\pi_i : \PP^n_{V_i}\to V_i$ the projection. 
1959	As the higher cohomologies of all fibers (in particular, the first
1960	cohomology) vanish by \textbf{(B)}, 
1961	it follows by semi-continuity theory for the flat family $\F_{V_i}$
1962	over $V_i$ that for any $s\in V_i$ 
1963	the base change homomorphism
1964	$$({\pi_i}_*\F_{V_i}(m))|_s \to  H^0(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))$$
1965	is an isomorphism for $m\ge r_i$. Taking $N$ to be the maximum of
1966	all $r_i$ over the finitely many non-empty $V_i$, and composing the
1967	above two base change isomorphisms, we get the following.
1968	
1969	
1970	\medskip
1971	
1972	%\statement\label{base change for direct image} 
1973	\textbf{(C) } There exists an integer $N \ge N_1$ such that 
1974	the base change homomorphism
1975	$$(\pi_*\F(m))|_s \to  H^0(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))$$
1976	is an isomorphism for all $m\ge N$ and $s\in S$. 
1977	
1978	
1979	
1980	\medskip
1981	
1982	
1983	\textbf{Note } We now forget the subschemes $V_i$ but retain the facts 
1984	\textbf{(A)}, %\ref{finitely many Hilbert polynomials},
1985	\textbf{(B)}, %\ref{uniform vanishing of higher cohomology} 
1986	\textbf{(C)} %\ref{base change for direct image} 
1987	which were proved using the $V_i$.
1988	
1989	\medskip
1990	
1991	
1992	Let $\pi : \PP^n_S \to S$ denote the projection. Consider the 
1993	coherent sheaves $E_0, \ldots, E_n$ on $S$, defined by
1994	$$E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\mbox{ for } i=0,\ldots,n.$$

49	# Reference Retriever
50	
51	You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).
52	
53	The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.
54	
55	## Your Job
56	
57	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
58	
59	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).
60	2. **Download the original file(s)** with `curl`/`wget` via `Bash` into `references/`. Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when it is openly available, AND keep the PDF too.
61	3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
62	4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
63	5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
64	6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
65	
66	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.
67

197	  $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))$ via the affine-base case
198	  (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
199	  base-change identification $F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}$
200	  (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_base_change}). Each $F_i \hookrightarrow F$ is an open
201	  subfunctor (pull back $U_i$ along $f$). The $F_i$ jointly cover $F$ since the $U_i$
202	  cover $X$. The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
203	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$
204	  of Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
205	\end{proof}
206	
207	\section{Affine base}
208	\label{sec:relspec_affine}
209	
210	\begin{theorem}[Relative spectrum over an affine base]
211	  \label{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}
212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff}
213	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras, thm:relative_spec_exists}
214	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine)
215	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L491-L545).
216	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
217	  % "In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
218	  %  Let $F$ be the functor associated to $(S, \mathcal{A})$ above.
219	  %  If $S$ is affine, then $F$ is representable by the
220	  %  affine scheme $\Spec(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{A}))$."
221	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine).}
222	  Let $X = \Spec R$ be an affine scheme and let $\mathcal{A}$ be a quasi-coherent
223	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra. Set $A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})$, regarded as an
224	  $R$-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of $X$-schemes
225	  \[
226	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
227	  \]
228	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
229	  spectrum of the global sections.
230	\end{theorem}
231	
232	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write $S = \Spec(R)$ and $A = \Gamma(S, \mathcal{A})$.
233	% Then $A$ is an $R$-algebra and $\mathcal{A} = \widetilde A$.
234	% The ring map $R \to A$ gives rise to a canonical map
235	% $$
236	% f_{univ} : \Spec(A)
237	% \longrightarrow
238	% S = \Spec(R).
239	% $$
240	% We have
241	% $f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} =  \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}$
242	% by Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}.
243	% Hence there is a canonical map
244	% $$
245	% \varphi_{univ} :
246	% f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
247	% \longrightarrow
248	% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
249	% $$
250	% coming from the $A$-module map $A \otimes_R A \to A$,
251	% $a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'$. We claim that the pair
252	% $(f_{univ}, \varphi_{univ})$ represents $F$ in this case."
253	\begin{proof}
254	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras, thm:relative_spec_univ}
255	  Write $X = \Spec R$ and $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})$. Quasi-coherence of
256	  $\mathcal{A}$ gives $\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}$ as $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
257	  (with the $R$-algebra structure on $A$ inducing the algebra structure on
258	  $\widetilde A$). The ring map $R \to A$ gives a morphism
259	  $f_{\mathrm{univ}} : \Spec A \to \Spec R = X$ and a canonical
260	  $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A}$-algebra map
261	  $\varphi_{\mathrm{univ}} : f_{\mathrm{univ}}^* \mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
262	  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
263	  $a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'$. We verify $(\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})$
264	  represents $F$ on the affine base: given a test scheme $T$ and a pair
265	  $(f, \varphi)$, the composite
266	  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
267	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$
268	  is a ring map $A \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$ and hence corresponds to a morphism
269	  $T \to \Spec A$ by the affine universal property
270	  (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.toSpec\_naturality} /
271	  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map
272	  $(f, \varphi) \mapsto a$ defined by $f = f_{\mathrm{univ}} \circ a$,
273	  $\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}$.
274	\end{proof}
275	
276	\section{Base change}
277	\label{sec:relspec_basechange}
278	
279	\begin{theorem}[Base change of the relative spectrum]
280	  \label{thm:relative_spec_base_change}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.base_change}
282	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras, thm:relative_spec_exists}
283	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LS (lemma-spec-base-change and
284	  % lemma-spec-properties part (2))
285	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L467-L489 + L662-L691).
286	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
287	  % "In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
288	  %  Let $F$ be the functor
289	  %  associated to $(S, \mathcal{A})$ above.
290	  %  Let $g : S' \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
291	  %  Set $\mathcal{A}' = g^*\mathcal{A}$. Let $F'$ be the
292	  %  functor associated to $(S', \mathcal{A}')$ above.
293	  %  Then there is a canonical isomorphism
294	  %  $$
295	  %  F' \cong h_{S'} \times_{h_S} F
296	  %  $$
297	  %  of functors. [...]
298	  %  For every morphism $g : S' \to S$ we have
299	  %  $S' \times_S \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) =
300	  %  \underline{\Spec}_{S'}(g^*\mathcal{A})$."
301	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LS (lemma-spec-base-change +
302	  lemma-spec-properties).}
303	  Let $g : T \to X$ be a morphism of schemes and let $\mathcal{A}$ be a quasi-coherent
304	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra. Then $g^* \mathcal{A}$ is a quasi-coherent
305	  $\mathcal{O}_T$-algebra and there is a canonical isomorphism of $T$-schemes
306	  \[
307	    T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\;
308	    \underline{\Spec}_T(g^* \mathcal{A}).
309	  \]
310	\end{theorem}
311	
312	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "A pair $(f' : T \to S', \varphi' : (f')^*\mathcal{A}' \to \mathcal{O}_T)$
313	% is the same as a pair $(f, \varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T)$
314	% together with a factorization of $f$ as $f = g \circ f'$. Namely with
315	% this notation we have
316	% $(f')^* \mathcal{A}' = (f')^*g^*\mathcal{A} = f^*\mathcal{A}$.
317	% Hence the lemma."
318	\begin{proof}
319	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_univ}
320	  By Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}, $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ represents
321	  the functor $F$ sending an $X$-scheme $h : S \to X$ to the set of
322	  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra maps $h^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S$. A morphism
323	  $S \to T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ over $T$ is the same as a pair of an
324	  $X$-morphism $S \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ together with a $T$-factorisation
325	  of $S \to X$, i.e.\ a morphism $S \to T$ whose composition with $g$ equals
326	  $S \to X$. Tracing through, this is a $T$-morphism $f' : S \to T$ together with an
327	  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra map $(f')^*(g^*\mathcal{A}) = f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S$.
328	  This is precisely $F'(S)$, the functor for $(T, g^*\mathcal{A})$, so the canonical map
329	  $T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to \underline{\Spec}_T(g^*\mathcal{A})$
330	  is an isomorphism by Yoneda.
331	\end{proof}
332	
333	\section{Functoriality}
334	\label{sec:relspec_functoriality}
335	
336	\begin{theorem}[Relative spectrum as a functor]
337	  \label{thm:relative_spec_functorial}
338	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.functor}
339	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras, thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_univ}
340	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LR (definition-relative-spec +
341	  % lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec + lemma-spec-properties part (3))
342	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L602-L691).
343	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
344	  % "Let $S$ be a scheme. Let $\mathcal{A}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf of
345	  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras. The {\it relative spectrum of $\mathcal{A}$ over
346	  %  $S$}, or simply the {\it spectrum of $\mathcal{A}$ over $S$} is the scheme
347	  %  constructed in Lemma \ref{lemma-glue-relative-spec} which represents the
348	  %  functor $F$ (\ref{equation-spec}), see
349	  %  Lemma \ref{lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec}.
350	  %  We denote it $\pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \to S$.
351	  %  The ``universal family'' is a morphism of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras
352	  %  $$
353	  %  \mathcal{A}
354	  %  \longrightarrow
355	  %  \pi_*\mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})}
356	  %  $$
357	  %  [...]
358	  %  The universal map
359	  %  $$
360	  %  \mathcal{A}
361	  %  \longrightarrow
362	  %  \pi_*\mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})}
363	  %  $$
364	  %  is an isomorphism of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras."
365	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LR (definition-relative-spec +
366	  lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec + lemma-spec-properties);
367	  [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
368	  The construction $\mathcal{A} \mapsto \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ assembles into
369	  a contravariant functor
370	  \[
371	    \underline{\Spec}_X : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AffSch}/X
372	  \]
373	  from quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras to affine $X$-schemes: a morphism of
374	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras $\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ induces, by the universal
375	  property (Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) applied to the pair
376	  $(\pi_\mathcal{B}, \psi \circ \pi_\mathcal{B}^*) :
377	  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{A} \to \pi_\mathcal{B}^*\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec\mathcal{B}}$,
378	  an $X$-morphism
379	  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\psi) : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$.
380	  Functoriality (identity, composition) is direct from the universal property. The
381	  pushforward $\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}$
382	  exhibits $\underline{\Spec}_X$ as an equivalence between $\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}$ and
383	  the full subcategory of affine $X$-schemes, with inverse $f \mapsto f_* \mathcal{O}_Y$
384	  (Stacks lemma-spec-properties part~(3)).
385	\end{theorem}
386	
387	\begin{proof}
388	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_univ, thm:relative_spec_affine_base}
389	  Functoriality follows directly from Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}: for
390	  $\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$, the pair
391	  $(\pi_\mathcal{B} : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to X, \;
392	  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*(\psi))$ defines an $X$-morphism
393	  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ via the
394	  representing universal pair. Identity and composition follow from naturality of the
395	  representing bijection in $\mathcal{A}$. For the equivalence claim, $\underline{\Spec}_X$
396	  is fully faithful because the isomorphism
397	  $\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}$
398	  (locally identifying with $\Gamma(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)),\mathcal{O}) = \mathcal{A}(U)$
399	  via Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) reconstructs $\mathcal{A}$ from
400	  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$. Essential surjectivity onto affine $X$-schemes uses
401	  the canonical morphism $X \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\pi_*\mathcal{O}_X)$ of Stacks
402	  lemma-canonical-morphism; on an affine $X$-scheme $\pi : Y \to X$, this canonical
403	  morphism is an isomorphism (affine-locally it is the identity
404	  $\Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y) \to \Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y)$).
405	\end{proof}
406	
407	\section{Lean encoding}
408	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
409	
410	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
411	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
412	$U \subseteq X$ with $U = \Spec R$ and $A := \mathcal{A}(U)$ regarded as an
413	$R$-algebra, the local piece is $\Spec A$ with structure morphism $\Spec A \to \Spec R = U$
414	induced by the algebra unit $R \to A$. Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
415	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
416	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of $\mathcal{A}$ via
417	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
418	$\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)$ for $V = \Spec S \subseteq U$).
419	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
420	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
421	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
422	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
423	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
424	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
425	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
426	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
427	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_base_change}) and functoriality
428	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_functorial}) are derived from the universal property by
429	Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
430	construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.
431	
432	\section{Out of scope}
433	\label{sec:relspec_out_of_scope}
434	
435	This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its three core
436	properties. The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters and
437	are explicitly out of scope here:

443	    \texttt{Picard\_LineBundlePullback.tex} (planned).

33213	\label{section-open-flat}
33214	
33215	\noindent
33216	We use Lemma \ref{lemma-colimit-eventually-flat} to reduce to the
33217	Noetherian case. The Noetherian case is handled using the characterization
33218	of exact complexes given in Section \ref{section-complex-exact}.
33219	
33220	\begin{lemma}
33221	\label{lemma-CM-dim-finite-type}
33222	Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type
33223	$k$-algebra. Let $f_1, \ldots, f_i$ be elements
33224	of $S$. Assume that $S$ is Cohen-Macaulay and
33225	equidimensional of dimension $d$, and that
33226	$\dim V(f_1, \ldots, f_i) \leq d - i$. Then equality
33227	holds and $f_1, \ldots, f_i$ forms a regular
33228	sequence in $S_{\mathfrak q}$ for every prime $\mathfrak q$
33229	of $V(f_1, \ldots, f_i)$.
33230	\end{lemma}
33231	
33232	\begin{proof}
33233	If $S$ is Cohen-Macaulay and equidimensional of dimension
33234	$d$, then we have $\dim(S_{\mathfrak m}) = d$ for all maximal
33235	ideals $\mathfrak m$ of $S$, see
33236	Lemma \ref{lemma-disjoint-decomposition-CM-algebra}.
33237	By Proposition \ref{proposition-CM-module} we see that
33238	for all maximal ideals $\mathfrak m \in V(f_1, \ldots, f_i)$
33239	the sequence is a regular sequence in $S_{\mathfrak m}$ and
33240	the local ring $S_{\mathfrak m}/(f_1, \ldots, f_i)$ is
33241	Cohen-Macaulay of dimension $d - i$. This actually
33242	means that $S/(f_1, \ldots, f_i)$ is Cohen-Macaulay
33243	and equidimensional of dimension $d - i$.
33244	\end{proof}
33245	
33246	\begin{lemma}
33247	\label{lemma-open-regular-sequence}
33248	Let $R \to S$ be a finite type ring map. Let $d$ be an integer such that all
33249	fibres $S \otimes_R \kappa(\mathfrak p)$ are Cohen-Macaulay and equidimensional
33250	of dimension $d$. Let $f_1, \ldots, f_i$ be elements of $S$. The set
33251	$$
33252	\{ \mathfrak q \in V(f_1, \ldots, f_i)
33253	\mid f_1, \ldots, f_i
33254	\text{ are a regular sequence in }
33255	S_{\mathfrak q}/\mathfrak p S_{\mathfrak q}
33256	\text{ where }\mathfrak p = R \cap \mathfrak q
33257	\}
33258	$$
33259	is open in $V(f_1, \ldots, f_i)$.
33260	\end{lemma}
33261	
33262	\begin{proof}
33263	Write $\overline{S} = S/(f_1, \ldots, f_i)$.
33264	Suppose $\mathfrak q$ is an element of the set defined in the
33265	lemma, and $\mathfrak p$ is the corresponding prime of $R$.
33266	We will use relative dimension as defined in
33267	Definition \ref{definition-relative-dimension}.
33268	First, note that $d = \dim_{\mathfrak q}(S/R) =
33269	\dim(S_{\mathfrak q}/\mathfrak pS_{\mathfrak q}) +
33270	\text{trdeg}_{\kappa(\mathfrak p)}\ \kappa(\mathfrak q)$
33271	by Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}.
33272	Since $f_1, \ldots, f_i$ form a regular sequence in the
33273	Noetherian local ring $S_{\mathfrak q}/\mathfrak pS_{\mathfrak q}$
33274	Lemma \ref{lemma-one-equation} tells us that
33275	$\dim(\overline{S}_{\mathfrak q}/\mathfrak p\overline{S}_{\mathfrak q})
33276	= \dim(S_{\mathfrak q}/\mathfrak pS_{\mathfrak q}) - i$.
33277	We conclude that $\dim_{\mathfrak q}(\overline{S}/R)
33278	= \dim(\overline{S}_{\mathfrak q}/\mathfrak p\overline{S}_{\mathfrak q}) +
33279	\text{trdeg}_{\kappa(\mathfrak p)}\ \kappa(\mathfrak q)
33280	= d - i$ by Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}.
33281	By Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-fibres-bounded-open-upstairs}
33282	we have $\dim_{\mathfrak q'}(\overline{S}/R) \leq d - i$
33283	for all $\mathfrak q' \in V(f_1, \ldots, f_i) = \Spec(\overline{S})$
33284	in a neighbourhood of $\mathfrak q$. Thus after replacing
33285	$S$ by $S_g$ for some $g \in S$, $g \not \in \mathfrak q$

33287	$\mathfrak q'$. The result follows from Lemma
33288	\ref{lemma-CM-dim-finite-type}.
33289	\end{proof}

33292	\label{lemma-exact-on-fibres-open}
